pages.title: Overview
pages.body:
A 20×20 grid of emissive flat planes face the camera. Every cell computes its own hue from its grid coordinates and the global elapsed time, producing a slow chromatic wave that rolls across the field. The scene uses no textures — all color is driven by math.

Navigate through the pages below to walk through each section of this file.

pages.title: HSL Helper Functions
pages.body:
hslToRgb() implements the standard HSL-to-RGB formula using a helper hue() that wraps fractional input into [0, 1] and applies the piecewise linear conversion. hslToHex() clamps each channel, scales to 0–255, and bit-packs the result into a single integer suitable for Three.js setHex().

· Keeping the math in two small pure functions makes it easy to call from both setup() and update()
· Clamping before scaling prevents overflow from the wave offsets added in update()

pages.title: Grid Creation
pages.body: The nested row/col loop computes a normalized offset (nx, ny) centered around zero, then derives an initial hue from the cell's position. Each tile is a 1×1 plane with a black base color — all visible color comes from the emissive channel. The col and row indices are stored in userData so update() can recompute the position offset without iterating a 2D array.

pages.title: Per-Frame Color Wave
pages.body: Each frame, a sine wave keyed on elapsed time and the tile's (nx, ny) coordinates perturbs the base hue. The base hue itself advances linearly with elapsed * 0.06, so the palette scrolls slowly through the full color wheel. Lightness also oscillates via a second sine with different spatial frequencies, adding a brightness ripple that runs diagonally across the grid.

pages.title: Teardown
pages.body: No explicit teardown is needed. All tile meshes were registered with ctx.add() and are disposed automatically by the Artlab runtime when the example unloads.
